items.prompt: A search uses a closed active interval [left, right]. Which loop condition processes a one-element interval?

feedback.reason: A closed interval remains non-empty while left is less than or equal to right.
feedback.details: When left equals right, exactly one candidate remains and must still be examined.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_lt_right: This stops before processing the final candidate when left equals right.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_lt_left: That describes an empty closed interval.

items.prompt: A search uses a half-open active interval [left, right). Which loop condition continues while at least one candidate remains?

feedback.reason: A half-open interval is empty exactly when its boundaries are equal.
feedback.details: Its candidate count is right - left, so positive length corresponds to left < right.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_le_right: When left equals right, the half-open interval is empty.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_lt_left: That is not the normal non-empty condition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_eq_right: This identifies the exit state, not the active state.

items.prompt: In a half-open first-true search, predicate(mid) is true and mid may be the first true index. Which update preserves mid as a candidate?

feedback.reason: The search seeks the leftmost true value, so a true midpoint belongs to the retained right boundary.
feedback.details: Using a half-open interval [left, right), setting right = mid keeps mid and discards only positions strictly to its right.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_mid_minus_one: This can discard the first true index itself.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_mid_plus_one: This discards mid and every earlier candidate.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.return_mid: A true midpoint may not be the first true position.

items.prompt: A loop uses lower-biased mid = floor((left + right) / 2) and the update left = mid when the answer lies to the right. What risk exists when right = left + 1?

options.text: mid equals left, so the interval may not shrink.
The only risk is integer overflow.
The loop necessarily skips every candidate.

feedback.reason: A nonterminal update must strictly reduce the active interval.
feedback.details: With two adjacent candidates, lower-biased mid equals left. Retaining left unchanged repeats the same state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.overflow_only: Even overflow-safe arithmetic can still fail to progress.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.skip_all: The immediate defect is lack of strict progress.

items.prompt: Which midpoint bias supports a closed last-true search that updates left = mid?

options.text: Use an upper-biased midpoint such as left + (right - left + 1) / 2.

feedback.reason: Upper bias makes mid equal right when two adjacent candidates remain.
feedback.details: Then left = mid strictly moves the lower boundary and preserves a potentially valid midpoint.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_mid: With two candidates, mid equals left and left = mid does not progress.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.outside_mid: The midpoint must remain inside the active interval.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.random_mid: Correctness and progress need a deterministic in-range rule.

items.prompt: What does the exit state left == right mean in a correctly defined half-open lower-bound search?

options.text: It is the unique insertion or first-valid boundary determined by the invariant.
The algorithm has failed because the interval is empty.
left is merely the last midpoint visited.

feedback.reason: The search converges to the boundary separating known-invalid and candidate-valid positions.
feedback.details: A correct invariant gives the empty interval a precise post-loop meaning even when no exact match exists.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_match: The target may be absent and left may equal n.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.invalid_state: The empty interval is the intended convergence state.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_mid: Its meaning comes from the maintained partition invariant.

items.prompt: Which interval representation naturally allows right = n as an initial boundary for an n-element array?

feedback.reason: The exclusive right boundary may equal the array length.
feedback.details: This convention represents the full index set while keeping every actual access below n.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.closed: Index n would be included even though it is outside the array.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.singleton: Interval length is unrelated to this representation choice.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rotated: Half-open boundaries are a general convention.

items.prompt: A developer mixes loop condition left < right with closed-interval updates right = mid - 1 and left = mid + 1. What is the primary concern?

options.text: The condition and updates may skip the final candidate because they come from incompatible interval contracts.
The mixed form is always faster.
The issue occurs only with duplicate values.
The variable mid needs a different name.

feedback.reason: Loop condition, midpoint inclusion, updates, and exit meaning must belong to one coherent boundary contract.
feedback.details: Borrowing pieces from different templates can silently exclude a valid endpoint.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_faster: Speed does not repair an off-by-one defect.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_only: It can fail on distinct singleton intervals.

items.prompt: Which components form a coherent closed-interval exact-search contract? Select all that apply.

options.text: After a strict mismatch, update to mid + 1 or mid - 1.
Treat right = n as an included valid array index.

feedback.reason: A closed interval includes both boundaries and removes a mismatching midpoint explicitly.
feedback.details: The initial right boundary must be the final valid index.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_n: Index n is outside the array.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.loop_closed: A singleton closed interval remains active.

items.prompt: Which components form a coherent half-open first-valid search? Select all that apply.

options.text: Initialize the full domain as [0, n).
On a true midpoint, set right = mid.
On a false midpoint, set left = mid + 1.

feedback.reason: This contract converges to the first true or insertion boundary.
feedback.details: Both updates strictly shrink [left, right) while preserving the possible boundary.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.initial_half_open: right = n is an exclusive sentinel.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.loop_half_open: Equal boundaries mean an empty interval.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.keep_true_mid: Mid may be the first valid position.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.discard_false_mid: Mid cannot be the first true value.

items.prompt: Which properties must every nonterminal binary-search update satisfy? Select all that apply.

options.text: Retain every candidate that can still satisfy the result contract.
Remove only candidates proven impossible by midpoint evidence.

feedback.reason: Correct updates combine semantic preservation with mechanical progress.
feedback.details: Retaining mid is legal only in branches where mid may still be the boundary and the midpoint bias guarantees shrinkage.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.keep_mid_always: A known-invalid midpoint should be removed and may cause stalling if always retained.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.preserve_answer: Correctness requires the answer remain inside the active domain.

items.prompt: Which tests are especially useful for boundary-contract review? Select all that apply.

options.text: Only one large case whose answer begins at the midpoint.

feedback.reason: The smallest domains expose off-by-one and non-progress defects directly.
feedback.details: Large midpoint hits are poor evidence that the interval contract is coherent.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_large_middle: This may never exercise endpoint or convergence defects.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.empty: It tests the initial and exit representation.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.two_candidates: It exposes midpoint-bias and progress errors.

items.prompt: Arrange the process for designing a binary-search boundary contract.

elements.text: Define the exact result: any match, first valid, last valid, or insertion point.
Choose closed or half-open active-domain semantics.
Derive midpoint bias and updates that preserve the result candidate.
State the precise meaning of the loop exit boundaries.

feedback.reason: Boundary code should be derived from the result contract rather than memorized independently.
feedback.details: The exit state becomes trustworthy only when every earlier design choice is coherent.

items.prompt: Arrange one closed-interval exact-search iteration.

elements.text: Confirm left <= right.
Choose mid inside [left, right].
Compare the midpoint value with target.
Return on equality; otherwise exclude mid and the impossible side.

feedback.reason: The midpoint is read only inside a non-empty closed interval.
feedback.details: Strict mismatches remove mid because exact equality has already failed.

items.prompt: Arrange one half-open first-true iteration.

elements.text: Confirm left < right.
Compute a midpoint in [left, right).
Evaluate the monotonic predicate at mid.
Set right = mid if true; otherwise set left = mid + 1.

feedback.reason: A false midpoint is removed, while a true midpoint remains a possible first true.
feedback.details: Both branches strictly reduce the half-open interval.

items.prompt: Arrange one closed last-true iteration using an upper-biased midpoint.

elements.text: Confirm left < right while more than one candidate remains.
Compute mid biased toward right.
Evaluate whether mid is valid.
If valid set left = mid; otherwise set right = mid - 1.

feedback.reason: Upper bias makes left = mid a strictly progressing update.
feedback.details: The invariant retains the greatest valid candidate until left and right converge.

items.prompt: Arrange the reasoning used to prove strict progress.

elements.text: Express the active-domain size from the chosen interval convention.
Bound mid relative to left and right, including two-candidate cases.
Show every nonterminal update makes the size smaller.
Conclude repeated updates must reach the exit state.

feedback.reason: Termination follows from a nonnegative integer measure that strictly decreases.
feedback.details: The two-candidate state is where midpoint-bias defects most often appear.

items.prompt: Arrange the debugging steps for a binary-search infinite loop.

elements.text: Record left, mid, and right on repeated iterations.
Find the branch that leaves both boundaries unchanged.
Check whether midpoint bias matches the branch that retains mid.
Repair the update and retest one- and two-candidate domains.

feedback.reason: A repeated boundary triple proves missing progress.
feedback.details: The repair must be validated against the smallest domains rather than only the original large input.

items.prompt: Arrange the interpretation of a completed half-open lower-bound search.

elements.text: The loop exits with left == right.
All positions strictly before left are known to fail the boundary predicate.
Every position at or after left is valid or outside the array sentinel.
Return left as the insertion or first-valid boundary.

feedback.reason: The returned index is justified by a partition invariant, not by a final equality check.
feedback.details: It may legally equal n when no array position satisfies the predicate.

items.prompt: Arrange the checks for converting a closed exact-search template into a half-open boundary search.

elements.text: Redefine right as an exclusive boundary.
Initialize right to n rather than n - 1.
Use left < right and derive mid-retaining updates from the boundary predicate.
Define what the converged insertion boundary means.

feedback.reason: Changing one line is insufficient because interval semantics affect every part of the algorithm.
feedback.details: A safe conversion updates initialization, loop condition, branch inclusion, and post-loop interpretation together.

items.prompt: A coherent iterative binary search halves an active domain of n candidates and performs O(1) work per iteration. Select its iteration bound and auxiliary-space complexity.
constraints: Every nonterminal branch strictly reduces the domain to at most about half.

feedback.reason: Boundary convention changes constants and exit meaning, not the asymptotic effect of halving.
feedback.details: Constant boundary variables suffice for an iterative implementation.

items.prompt: A recursive binary search halves the domain until one candidate remains. Select the recursion depth and stack-space complexity.
constraints: Each call retains one stack frame.
The active-domain size is reduced by about half.

feedback.reason: Logarithmic convergence creates logarithmic recursive depth.
feedback.details: An iterative version can retain the same boundary invariant with constant auxiliary space.
